speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. This episode is a practical companion you can keep beside you while working on GitHub with a screen reader. It pairs especially well with the earlier lessons on understanding GitHub, navigating repositories, and the VS Code interface. And one administrative note up front: there is also a listen-first-or-review-later version of this appendix in the course podcast list, so learners can preview the concepts before reading or use the audio afterward to reinforce them.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less of a theory episode and more of a working reference?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Think of it as an audio cheat sheet for GitHub navigation with NVDA, JAWS, and VoiceOver. The big promise is keyboard-only navigation, no mouse required. And the advice in the written companion is to keep the sheet open in a second window or print it, because the commands are organized by task.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that matters for different kinds of learners, right? Not just screen reader users.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For screen reader users, the sheet itself is designed to be navigated by heading, and the Elements List or Rotor can show all the sections at once. Tables are used heavily, with commands on the left and actions on the right. For low-vision learners, the recommendation is side-by-side reference, zooming to around one hundred fifty to two hundred percent, and noticing that key names are visually marked in code formatting. For sighted keyboard users, the guidance is to scan task-based sections, use the grouped command reference by screen reader, and bookmark the page as a fast reference.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before we do anything on GitHub, we need the core mental model: two screen reader modes on the web.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the part that confuses people when a key suddenly does one thing on one page and something completely different in a text box.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. First, Browse Mode, also called Virtual Cursor or Reading Mode. This is the mode for reading and moving through a web page. In this mode, the screen reader captures keys and turns them into navigation commands. So pressing H does not type the letter h. It jumps to the next heading.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do learners know they are in that mode?

speaker: ALEX
text: With NVDA, there is a lower-pitched sound when switching, and NVDA may announce browse mode. With JAWS, you may hear virtual cursor on. VoiceOver on macOS works a bit differently. It does not use the same browse-versus-focus toggle language in the same way. Instead, you rely on VoiceOver commands and interaction.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the other mode is for typing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Focus Mode, sometimes called Forms Mode or Application Mode. This is where keystrokes go to the web page itself, not to the screen reader's web navigation system. You need it for text fields, comment boxes, issue titles, pull request descriptions, and interactive controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the toggles?

speaker: ALEX
text: For NVDA, NVDA plus Space, which is often Insert plus Space or Caps Lock plus Space depending on your setup. For JAWS, JAWS plus Z, usually Insert plus Z, though forms mode may also switch automatically. For VoiceOver, the common action is VO plus Shift plus Down Arrow to interact with an element, and VO plus Shift plus Up Arrow to stop interacting.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the rule of thumb is simple.

speaker: ALEX
text: Browse mode to navigate. Focus mode to type.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's build your navigation toolkit. In Browse Mode, single-letter quick navigation is your best friend on GitHub. And for VoiceOver users, many of these same letters work when Quick Nav is enabled, while the Rotor remains a major navigation tool.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If someone memorizes only one category, which should it be?

speaker: ALEX
text: Headings. GitHub pages are structured with headings, and heading navigation is often the fastest way to move. H goes to the next heading. Shift plus H goes to the previous heading. Number keys let you jump by heading level: 1 for the next level-one heading, 2 for level two, 3 for level three, 4 for level four, and so on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why does that matter specifically on GitHub?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because page titles, section labels, issue titles, pull request titles, and commit headings are often marked with heading levels. The cheat sheet even calls out that issue and pull request titles are commonly level-three headings, and commit titles in pull requests can appear as level-four headings. So if you know the structure, you can move fast.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about jumping across page regions instead of content items?

speaker: ALEX
text: That is landmark navigation. On NVDA and JAWS-style browse navigation, D goes to the next landmark, and Shift plus D goes to the previous one. JAWS also commonly uses R for the next region. VoiceOver users usually open the Rotor with VO plus U and choose Landmarks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And GitHub uses landmarks a lot.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. Common landmarks include the navigation menu with your avatar and global navigation, repository navigation with tabs like Code, Issues, Pull Requests, and Actions, the main landmark for primary content, repository files navigation near the README or files area, pull requests list, search results list, add a comment, and pagination.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds incredibly useful when a page feels huge.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. Landmarks let you skip broad regions before refining with headings or links.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about links, buttons, and form fields?

speaker: ALEX
text: K moves to the next link in many browse systems, with Shift plus K for previous. B moves to the next button, Shift plus B for previous. F, and sometimes E depending on the reader, moves to the next form field or edit field. Tab and Shift plus Tab move among interactive elements in general. Enter or Space activates a control, and VoiceOver often uses VO plus Space to activate.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And lists and tables?

speaker: ALEX
text: Also important. L goes to the next list. I goes to the next list item. T goes to the next table. That matters a lot on GitHub because repository file listings are often table-like structures, and once you are in a table, control plus alt with arrow keys is the classic navigation pattern for moving between cells and rows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I am trying to inspect files in a repository, T might get me there faster than tabbing forever.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now for one of the most powerful features in this whole appendix: the Elements List, or the Rotor in VoiceOver.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is the tool people forget exists, and then once they learn it, everything changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very true. It opens a list of major page elements so you can jump directly where you need to go. In NVDA, use NVDA plus F7. In JAWS, Insert plus F6 opens the headings list, and Insert plus F7 opens the links list. In VoiceOver, VO plus U opens the Rotor, where you can switch among headings, links, landmarks, form controls, tables, and more.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Walk us through the actual workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Open the list or Rotor. Move between element categories, such as Headings, Links, Buttons, Form Controls, or Landmarks. Type to filter if your screen reader supports that list filtering. For example, type new issue to find a New Issue button or link quickly. Then press Enter, or the VoiceOver activation command, to jump there.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is not just a nice feature. It is a navigation superpower on complex pages.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly the phrase the appendix uses.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's make this concrete by reviewing the per-screen-reader command references.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Start with NVDA.

speaker: ALEX
text: On Windows with NVDA, browse-mode single-key navigation includes H and Shift plus H for next and previous heading, 1 through 6 for heading levels, K for link, B for button, F or E for form field, T for table, D for landmark, L for list, and I for list item.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And reading and mode control?

speaker: ALEX
text: Insert plus Space toggles Browse and Focus modes. Insert plus F7 opens the Elements List. NVDA plus Down Arrow starts reading from the cursor, often called Say All. NVDA plus Up Arrow reads the current line. Control stops reading.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now JAWS.

speaker: ALEX
text: JAWS on Windows has very similar virtual-cursor navigation: H and Shift plus H for headings, 1 through 6 for heading levels, U for next unvisited link, K for next link, B for next button, F for next form field, T for next table, R for next landmark region, L for next list, and I for next list item.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the mode and reading commands?

speaker: ALEX
text: Insert plus Z toggles the Virtual PC Cursor, which is the browse-versus-focus style control. Numpad Plus exits forms mode and reopens the PC cursor. Insert plus F6 is the headings list. Insert plus F7 is the links list. Insert plus Down Arrow starts reading from cursor. Insert plus Up Arrow reads the current line. Control stops reading.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And VoiceOver on macOS.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, VoiceOver itself starts or stops with Command plus F5. The VoiceOver modifier, abbreviated VO, is Control plus Option. VO plus U opens the Rotor. VO plus Right and VO plus Left move forward or backward. VO plus Space activates the current element. VO plus Shift plus Down Arrow interacts with an element, which is often how you effectively enter a focus-like mode. VO plus Shift plus Up Arrow stops interacting. VO plus A reads from the cursor. Control stops reading.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The appendix also gives study advice here, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Practice by page type. Don't try to memorize everything at once. Move to the section matching the GitHub page you are on, read each table left to right as goal then key sequence, and rehearse one workflow at a time. For low-vision learners, the compact tables are meant to remain readable at high zoom, and the key sequences are visually distinguished.

speaker: JAMIE
text: All right. Let's get into actual GitHub pages. When someone lands on a repository main page, what should they expect?

speaker: ALEX
text: A repository page usually has a page title heading, repository navigation tabs such as Code, Issues, Pull Requests, Actions, and sometimes Projects or Wiki, then a main content region with the file list and README. A very practical strategy is: first jump to the main landmark, then use heading navigation to orient, then jump to the repository navigation landmark if you need tabs, and use table navigation or links to move through the file list.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if the file list is hard to find?

speaker: ALEX
text: Try T for the next table, or use the Elements List or Rotor to find relevant headings and landmarks. Also, tabbing can work, but it is usually slower than jumping by structure.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about the issues list page?

speaker: ALEX
text: On an issues list, listen for the main region and page heading, then find the search results list or issues list area. Use headings to jump between sections, lists to identify the results area, and links to move among issue titles. If you need the New Issue control, the Elements List or Rotor is often the fastest way to locate it by name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And once you open a single issue?

speaker: ALEX
text: On the issue detail page, the issue title is a major anchor point. From there, you may navigate to metadata, the description, timeline items, comments, and eventually the Add a comment form. Headings help you move among major sections. Landmarks can jump you to the main content or comment area. And when you reach the comment box or another edit field, you switch into Focus Mode or interact mode before typing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Pull request lists are similar to issue lists?

speaker: ALEX
text: Very similar. You are generally moving through lists of pull request titles and status information. Again, headings, landmarks, and links are the fastest route, and the search results list or pull requests list landmark can be a strong anchor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then on a pull request detail page, things branch out into tabs.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The pull request detail page usually includes tabs such as Conversation, Commits, and Files Changed. The Conversation tab behaves a lot like an issue timeline, with the title, description, review activity, and comments. Headings and landmarks are your map. The Add a comment landmark or form field is especially important when you want to review or respond.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What changes on the Commits tab?

speaker: ALEX
text: The Commits tab often contains a list of commit entries, each of which may be exposed with heading-like structure or linked text. Heading-level navigation, lists, and links all help. The appendix specifically reminds learners that level-four headings may correspond to commit titles in pull requests, which can help if your reader supports heading-level jumps cleanly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the Files Changed tab is often the hardest one.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, because diffs and code views can be dense. Use headings and landmarks first to find the file or diff region, then use tables or structured navigation where available. This is also a place where GitHub feature previews or accessibility improvements may change the experience, so the appendix includes a specific note about checking and enabling feature previews.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How does that work?

speaker: ALEX
text: The core idea is that GitHub sometimes rolls out interface changes behind previews or feature flags. If the page does not match the instructions, check whether a preview is enabled or whether the interface has changed. For NVDA and JAWS users, browse mode navigation helps inspect settings pages and dialogs. For VoiceOver users, the Rotor and interaction commands are the main tools for reaching and changing those settings.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk about typing, because this is where many learners get tripped up.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. To enter text on GitHub, Focus Mode or interaction is required. On NVDA, that often means toggling with NVDA plus Space if it did not switch automatically. On JAWS, Insert plus Z or automatic forms mode may handle it. On VoiceOver, interact with the text area using VO plus Shift plus Down Arrow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And how do you know it worked?

speaker: ALEX
text: The most practical test is simple: type a letter. If the screen reader tries to use it as a navigation key, you are still in Browse Mode. If the letter goes into the field, you are in the right mode.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The appendix also mentions markdown shortcuts inside GitHub text areas.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. While the exact set can vary by platform and editor behavior, the important teaching point is that GitHub text areas support common markdown formatting workflows. So when you are in Focus Mode, you can use familiar keyboard shortcuts to format text efficiently. The key accessibility point is that those shortcuts only help once you are truly inside the editable field.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now, dropdown menus and flyouts.

speaker: JAMIE
text: These can be awkward because they look simple visually, but keyboard focus can jump around.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The general strategy is to move to the button that opens the menu, activate it, then navigate within the flyout using Tab, Shift plus Tab, arrow keys where supported, or screen-reader structural commands if the menu is exposed semantically. For VoiceOver users in particular, interacting with the menu or popover content may be necessary before its controls become easy to traverse.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So VoiceOver users should be ready to use interact and stop interacting as part of the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. That is explicitly called out.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's move into GitHub's own built-in keyboard shortcuts. These are separate from the screen reader's commands.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right, and that distinction matters. Screen reader commands move you through accessibility structure. GitHub shortcuts trigger GitHub actions. The art is using them together without conflict.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the overall advice?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use GitHub shortcuts alongside a screen reader thoughtfully. Make sure you are not in a text field unless the shortcut is intended for text editing. Be aware of Browse Mode intercepting letters. And when GitHub uses G-based shortcut sequences, there is a practical sequence to follow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does that mean?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub has multi-key shortcuts like pressing G and then another key to go somewhere. If your screen reader is in a mode where single letters are navigation commands, the initial G may never reach GitHub. So the practical sequence is: ensure you are in the right mode, often focus-like interaction on the page body or a state where GitHub can receive the keys, then press the GitHub shortcut sequence.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you forget what shortcuts are available?

speaker: ALEX
text: Press question mark on GitHub to open the shortcut dialog. That is a built-in discoverability feature. Then read the dialog with your screen reader like any other modal: use headings, read line by line, tab through controls, or use the Rotor or Elements List as appropriate.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The appendix breaks these shortcuts down by page type too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Site-wide shortcuts work on every GitHub page. Repository navigation shortcuts work on any repository page. There are also groups for source code browsing while viewing a file, issue and pull request lists, issue and pull request detail pages, comments while inside any text area with Focus Mode required, the Files Changed tab in pull requests, the Notifications page, GitHub Actions, and Projects in board and table view.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the teaching goal is not to memorize every GitHub shortcut from audio.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. The goal is to know that these categories exist, know that question mark reveals them, and know how to let GitHub receive the keys instead of your screen reader intercepting them.

speaker: ALEX
text: The appendix also provides quick-reference navigation patterns. These are habits you can repeat across pages.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Such as?

speaker: ALEX
text: A simple pattern is: jump to main, scan headings, use landmarks to change regions, open the Elements List or Rotor if you feel lost, move to the target form field, switch to Focus Mode to type, then return to Browse Mode to keep navigating. Another pattern is to use headings for orientation, links for selection, and tables for structured content like file lists.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That kind of pattern is reassuring because it scales across a lot of GitHub.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You do not need a different mental model for every page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now troubleshooting. This may be the most emotionally important part, because when things do not match the lesson, learners often assume they did something wrong.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, and often they did not. First issue: the page does not match the instructions. That can happen because GitHub's interface changes, experiments are rolled out gradually, or feature previews alter the layout. The advice is to reorient using headings and landmarks rather than expecting every control in exactly the same place, and to check feature preview settings if relevant.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Second problem: I'm hearing too much or navigating too slowly.

speaker: ALEX
text: That usually means you are reading linearly when structural navigation would be faster. Use H for headings, D or the Rotor for landmarks, T for tables, and especially the Elements List or Rotor to jump directly. Also remember the read-all and stop-reading commands so you can control pace.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Third: I pressed H but it's typing the letter h.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the classic mode mismatch. You are probably in Focus Mode or inside an editable field. Exit forms or interaction mode if your goal is navigation. On NVDA, toggle with NVDA plus Space. On JAWS, use Insert plus Z or leave forms mode, often with Numpad Plus. On VoiceOver, stop interacting with VO plus Shift plus Up Arrow if needed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Fourth: I can't find the comment box.

speaker: ALEX
text: Use landmarks like Add a comment, jump by form fields with F, search the Elements List or Rotor for comment-related controls, and remember the box may be below a long timeline. Once you reach it, switch into Focus Mode before typing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the code or diff area is hard to navigate.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is common. Start broader. Find the file or diff heading first, then move within the region using tables, links, or line-by-line reading as appropriate. If the layout seems unfamiliar, check whether a feature preview or interface change is affecting the page. And give yourself permission to use multiple strategies, not just one key.

speaker: ALEX
text: Near the end, the appendix points learners to official screen reader resources.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why include that in a course appendix?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because this sheet is practical, but it does not replace the official documentation for your screen reader or the platform standards behind accessible web behavior. Learners are encouraged to consult GitHub Docs, especially keyboard shortcut documentation, GitHub's changelog for interface updates, the W3C WCAG overview, WAI tutorials for accessible design patterns, and the WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if something behaves differently, those are part of your evidence trail.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The appendix also reminds learners that related keyboard shortcuts appear in other appendices, so this is one reference among several in the course.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there is a compatibility note too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Screen reader compatibility is never perfectly frozen. If a command does not work as described, possibilities include a different key map, laptop keyboard differences, Quick Nav being off, VoiceOver interaction state, a GitHub UI change, browser differences, or screen reader version changes. The key idea is not that you failed. It is that accessibility workflows are version-sensitive, and authoritative sources should be checked when behavior differs.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the appendix closes with an authoritative-source map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. It ties sections back to dependable references such as GitHub Docs, the GitHub changelog, WCAG overview pages, WAI tutorials, and WAI-ARIA practices. That is a reminder that this cheat sheet is grounded in broader accessibility guidance, not isolated tips.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If you had to leave learners with a compact practice routine after this episode, what would it be?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open a GitHub page. Jump to main. Use H to sample headings. Use D, R, or the Rotor to inspect landmarks. Open the Elements List or Rotor and practice jumping to a known target like New Issue or Add a comment. Enter Focus Mode only when it is time to type. Exit when it is time to navigate. And if GitHub's own shortcuts are needed, make sure GitHub, not the screen reader, receives the keystrokes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And emotionally?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not judge yourself by speed on the first pass. This is pattern learning. Once headings, landmarks, lists, tables, elements lists, and mode switching become automatic, GitHub becomes much more predictable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a great note to end on.

speaker: ALEX
text: Thanks for learning with us. Keep this cheat sheet nearby, practice on one page type at a time, and come back to it whenever a workflow feels rusty.
